positioning.positioning_summary: UC Berkeley’s MIMS is positioned as a two-year, full-time professional master’s for aspiring technology leaders and information professionals, with a strongly interdisciplinary curriculum spanning computer science, data systems/AI, human-centered design/UX, policy, ethics, law, and management. The program also signals a practical, career-oriented focus through hands-on project work, internships, and outcomes in UX, product, data, and tech leadership, while explicitly framing its work around ethical technology and social impact.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: MIMS is a two-year, full-time, 48-semester-unit professional degree. The first year is mostly a required core, followed by electives and additional breadth requirements in the second year, with a required final project in the last semester. The program is flexible overall and supports specialization through electives and thematic focus areas, but it does not present formal tracks or concentrations.

core_courses.course_summary: Required first-year core course introducing information organization and retrieval. Covers conceptual modeling, semantic representation, vocabulary and metadata design, classification, standardization, and information retrieval practices and applications.
learning_outcomes: Understand foundations of information organization and retrieval
Apply conceptual modeling and semantic representation
Design vocabularies, metadata, and classification schemes
Use information retrieval practices and related technologies
Analyze textual and non-textual information computationally

core_courses.course_summary: Required 3-unit core course introducing social issues around information and information technology, including technology’s impact on society and social groups.
learning_outcomes: Explain how information technologies affect social groups and social interaction.
Analyze how society shapes the technologies that are produced.
Discuss theories of technology in society.
Identify workplace, automation, and networked sociability issues.
Use social scientific methods to examine information technology problems.

core_courses.course_summary: Required first-year core course examining legal and ethical issues in information and communication technologies, including privacy, freedom of expression, consumer protection, cybersecurity, and copyright.
learning_outcomes: Analyze legal and ethical issues in information technologies.
Explain how policy frameworks shape technology design and use.
Evaluate privacy, copyright, and consumer protection concerns.
Reflect on the ethical impact of information professionals' work.

core_courses.course_summary: Required first-year core course in programming and computation. The 2-unit course introduces Python programming for computer science, data science, and information management, with emphasis on problem decomposition, program efficiency, and good programming style.
learning_outcomes: Write interactive Python programs
Analyze data and process text
Draw graphics and manipulate images
Simulate physical systems
Apply problem decomposition and efficient coding practices

core_courses.course_title: Introduction to Data Structures and Analytics
core_courses.course_summary: Required core course introducing data structures and data analysis in Python; 2 units; forms the second half of the INFO 206A/206B sequence.
learning_outcomes: Represent structured data sets
Manipulate and analyze data in Python
Apply best practices for writing code
Build on INFO 206A prerequisites

core_courses.course_title: Directed Group Work on Final Project
core_courses.course_summary: Required culminating final project completed in the fourth semester. The project integrates skills and concepts from the MIMS program and may take the form of a research paper or professionally oriented applied work.
learning_outcomes: Integrate skills and concepts learned in the MIMS program.
Formulate and carry out a sustained, coherent project.
Produce a tangible work product.
Manage a significant project from start to finish.
Present work in written and oral form.

electives.summary: Electives are broad and flexible, with students drawing from the I School catalog and other UC Berkeley departments to build specialization in areas such as UX, data science, policy, entrepreneurship, and privacy/security. The program requires at least 40 units from the Info course catalog, may include up to 6 units of individual or group study, and allows up to 8 units from other departments to count toward the degree.

positioning.positioning_summary: The Berkeley MDes is positioned as a three-semester, interdisciplinary professional graduate program in design for emerging technologies that connects technical rigor, design theory, and social practice, with strong emphasis on career preparation, real-world application, and socially responsible design.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: Berkeley MDes is a studio-based, three-semester professional master’s program completed over about 16 months and requiring a minimum of 39 units. The curriculum combines eight named required core courses, a summer Design@Large professional development experience, and four electives drawn from technical, entrepreneurship, social practice, and open UC Berkeley courses. The materials show a clear year-by-year sample sequence, but the program also allows some flexibility in elective timing and selection.

core_courses.course_summary: Foundational MDes core course that interweaves practical design methods with readings and lectures on the history of design and technology.
learning_outcomes: Use design methods to frame and solve problems
Apply noticing, observing, framing, and reframing
Connect design practice with history of design and technology
Develop critique and reflection skills across design contexts

core_courses.course_summary: Foundational course in design and technology frameworks. Students use human-centered design, research, concept generation, prototyping, critique, and a semester-long hardware-plus-digital project to create a product-service system.
learning_outcomes: Apply human-centered design methods to emerging technologies.
Generate and refine concepts through research and prototyping.
Build fluency with fabrication and microcontroller-based technologies.
Use critique and iteration to improve design solutions.
Create a product-service system addressing a well-defined need.

core_courses.course_summary: Colloquium-style course exploring a yearly design topic through invited lectures, readings, discussion, and written reflections.
learning_outcomes: Articulate new methods for connecting conceptual knowledge with technical skills.
Integrate ideas from multiple perspectives and worldviews.
Develop thoughtful questions and discussion points from advance readings.
Write reflective responses to lectures and discussions.
Engage contemporary design challenges at different scales of practice.

core_courses.course_summary: Introductory design studio that orients students to studio culture, iterative making, critique, communication, and portfolio development.
learning_outcomes: Understand the culture and life of a working studio
Develop ideas through feedback, critique, and refinement
Work with a variety of materials and media
Practice visual and verbal communication
Begin building a portfolio of design work

core_courses.course_summary: A substantial studio course focused on designing emerging technologies through iterative project work, critique, and technical production.
learning_outcomes: Design products, services, and experiences for emerging technologies
Iterate concepts through critique and refinement
Apply technical production skills in a studio context
Develop solutions to well-defined design problems
Integrate hardware and digital technologies in project work

core_courses.course_summary: Hands-on thesis studio focused on fabrication, building technologies, and fundamental design production skills in a studio environment.
learning_outcomes: Develop a thesis project in a studio setting
Apply fabrication and building technologies
Produce a real-world application or solution
Work with external partners or faculty-led themes
Advance fundamental design production skills

core_courses.course_summary: A 1-unit online seminar supporting the MDes thesis process and broader Design@Large reflection.
learning_outcomes: Develop an important thesis question for design.
Produce a formal written thesis document.
Connect socio-cultural investigation with technical exploration.
Reflect on and document the final studio project.
Situate thesis work within broader design practice.

core_courses.course_summary: Culminating MDes portfolio course in which students compile and reflect on at least four meaningful pieces of work, including studio, prior coursework, and Design@Large experience.
learning_outcomes: Compile a coherent portfolio of MDes work
Select projects that demonstrate key learning objectives
Present deeper investigations from prior classes
Reflect on studio and Design@Large experiences

electives.summary: The MDes requires four electives: one technical elective, one entrepreneurship or social practice elective, and two open electives. The approved lists are broad and cross-disciplinary, spanning computing, engineering, planning, new media, and design-related topics. The open electives may be any 3- or 4-unit upper-division or graduate UC Berkeley course, subject to availability. The structure supports individualized emphasis, but the program does not advertise formal tracks or concentrations.

positioning.positioning_summary: ArtCenter’s Media Design Practices MFA is positioned as an interdisciplinary, STEM-designated design program centered on emerging technologies, critical frameworks, and experimental studio practice. It emphasizes research, prototyping, and fieldwork, with explicit social-impact and global-context framing, and prepares students for professional roles at the intersection of technology, culture, and design.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: ArtCenter’s Media Design Practices MFA is an interdisciplinary, STEM-designated graduate program organized around a sequenced studio curriculum leading to thesis work. The evidence indicates two- and three-year pathways, with the three-year option adding preparatory undergraduate-level terms before the standard MGx track. The program emphasizes critical thinking, creative technology, prototyping, research, fieldwork, and thesis studio work; no distinct concentration tracks are clearly described in the fetched material.

Develop a reflective stance toward technology and society.
Connect design decisions to broader social and ethical contexts.

core_courses.course_summary: Fall-term thesis-writing and dissemination course in the Media Design Practices MFA. Students develop the written component of the thesis, situate their work in discourse, and prepare materials for sharing design practice through venues such as social media, journals, and biennales.
learning_outcomes: Develop the written component of a thesis
Use writing as a generative design tool
Situate work within critical discourse
Translate practice for public dissemination
Prepare thesis-related promotional materials

core_courses.course_summary: A required creative technology studio term in the MDP sequence, focused on emerging technologies, prototyping, and experimental making.
learning_outcomes: Investigate emerging technologies in design practice.

Use research and fieldwork to inform design decisions.

positioning.positioning_summary: Carnegie Mellon’s Computational Design program is positioned as a highly interdisciplinary, research-focused graduate program at the nexus of design and computation. It emphasizes critical technical practice, novel research contributions, and work that spans architecture, engineering, art, humanities, and computing, while also foregrounding emerging technologies, digital methods, physical fabrication, and socially and environmentally aware design inquiry.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: The Computational Design program is a two-year, research-focused MS/PhD pathway centered on interdisciplinary study at the intersection of design and computation. The evidence describes a lean sequence of research seminars, selective courses, special topics, independent studies, and a thesis, with students building individualized plans of study under faculty guidance. The program emphasizes technical, conceptual, historical, and critical inquiry, and the MS culminates in a substantial thesis.

electives.summary: Electives are highly flexible but guided by faculty and the program’s research orientation. The MS curriculum uses research seminars, selective courses, special topics seminars, independent studies, and a broad pool of eligible extra-departmental courses to let students build a personalized path; the evidence does not describe fixed elective buckets or named elective lists for this program page.

positioning.positioning_summary: The MDes in Design for Interactions is positioned as a rigorous, research-informed design program for students with prior design backgrounds and professional experience, centered on interaction design, systems thinking, design research, and thesis work. It emphasizes interdisciplinary collaboration, professional practice, and social impact through services, social innovation, sustainability, and future-oriented design.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: The Master of Design in Design for Interactions is a two-year, four-semester program built around required studios, seminars, labs, thesis preparation, and electives. The curriculum is described semester by semester, with first-year study focused on interaction, services, and social innovation, and second-year study centered on thesis research, development, and public defense. The page also indicates that students may take electives in the School of Design or across Carnegie Mellon, including outside design, to complement their skills and knowledge.

core_courses.course_summary: First-year core study in the MDes program introduces interaction design practice, design research, systems thinking, and methods for designing services and social innovation concepts.

Work with communities, organizations, and stakeholders
Develop a personal design voice through studio work

core_courses.course_summary: Project-based course on service design fundamentals, including analyzing and improving existing services and developing new service experiences. Students work across digital interactions and physical experiences, hypothesize, experiment, build, test, pivot, and pitch a service with value to a particular audience.
learning_outcomes: Understand the fundamentals of service design
Analyze and improve existing services
Integrate digital interactions with physical experiences
Use hypothesis-driven experimentation and testing
Pitch a service for a specific audience

positioning.positioning_summary: Harvard GSD’s Master in Design Studies is positioned as a research-based, interdisciplinary design program focused on urgent contemporary issues and social impact. curriculum.curriculum_summary: The Master in Design Studies is a four-semester, 72-unit program organized by domain (Ecologies, Mediums, Narratives, or Publics). The curriculum is flexible within each domain but still has a clear semester-by-semester structure.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: The M.Des. is a two-year, 54-credit program with a structured core and substantial elective flexibility. Required study includes a small set of core courses, one 4-credit choice from a specified course set, and a large elective block drawn mainly from 500- and 600-level IDN/IDX courses. Students without prior design preparation may need Foundation courses, and some international students may take English + Design courses concurrently in the first two semesters. positioning.positioning_summary: The mpd² program is positioned as a professional, industry-facing master’s for experienced practitioners who want to lead product design and development. Its messaging emphasizes cross-disciplinary training in business, design, technology, and product development, with a strong focus on emerging technology and AI systems, and on preparing graduates for senior leadership and go-to-market/product commercialization work.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: The mpd² curriculum is a cohort-based, highly sequenced program delivered in Fall 1 through Spring 2 terms, with 24 required courses plus a capstone project. electives.summary: The program offers a wide variety of electives, and students may also shape their studies through two areas of emphasis: Design or Research and Evaluation. The Design emphasis recommends electives such as accessibility, service design, conversational UX, web development, visual communication, digital product design, mobile interaction, ethnography, speculative design, emotional design, information visualization, sustainable interaction design, UX design systems, extended reality, and advanced projects. The Research and Evaluation emphasis recommends electives such as human-information interaction, human-centered research methods, data analysis, content strategy, advanced usability, practical ethnography, community building, digital analytics, audience research, and advanced projects. The page frames these electives as supporting focused career pathways rather than a fixed elective rule set.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: The program is a 30-credit master’s degree with multiple tracks. The Reading Instruction and Literacy Leadership track has a fixed set of seven 3-credit required courses plus 9 elective credits. positioning.positioning_summary: The program is positioned as a professional, research-informed industrial design degree that emphasizes interdisciplinary work, human-centered and ethically produced design, and strong connections to social impact and emerging practice areas such as design thinking, interfaces, interactions, and user experiences.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: The Industrial Design MFA/MDes curriculum is a structured 64-credit program built around a fixed six-course graduate sequence (ARTD 501–506) that moves from inquiry and ideation through implementation and thesis project development, followed by ARTD 599 thesis registration/completion. Students also complete approved design studio electives, an academic elective, and additional electives including seminars. The evidence describes a studio-based, research-informed curriculum with a strong thesis component and some flexibility in elective selection, but no distinct tracks within the Industrial Design degree itself.

electives.summary: Electives are partly structured rather than fully open: the program requires approved design studio electives from Art and Design, Architecture, or Engineering, plus an academic elective and additional electives that may include seminars. The evidence says students develop a plan of study with the graduate coordinator, and some elective credits are approved by that coordinator; for the MFA DRI, studio electives and free electives are drawn from across the School, the college, and the wider campus. These electives support individualized study and, in the DRI context, can align with the student’s research direction, but the Industrial Design degree itself does not describe formal tracks.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: The MSTI curriculum is a 21-month, full-time or hybrid, interdisciplinary program integrating engineering, business, and design. Evidence shows a structured sequence of required core courses and track-specific labs, plus a six-month capstone experience and optional summer experiences, with students choosing between connected devices and robotics tracks.

core_courses.course_summary: Credits: 4. Reviews intermediate object-oriented Python and applies it to data analysis, visualization, and manipulation.
learning_outcomes: Use intermediate object-oriented Python concepts
Apply Python to data analysis tasks
Create data visualizations
Manipulate and manage data effectively

core_courses.course_summary: Students learn to design, prototype, and fabricate physical artifacts using CAD and prototyping tools in the GIX labs.
learning_outcomes: Design physical artifacts using CAD and prototyping tools
Fabricate and test ideas in dedicated prototyping labs
Apply form-finding, fabrication, and assembly techniques
Use computer-controlled equipment to create complex parts and assemblies

core_courses.course_summary: Teaches strategies to build trust, foster commitment, define clear roles, and drive high performance in teams under volatile, uncertain, complex, and ambiguous conditions.
learning_outcomes: Build trust and commitment in team settings
Define clear roles and responsibilities
Generate buy-in for shared goals
Lead or contribute effectively in ambiguous conditions
Drive high performance in collaborative work

core_courses.course_summary: Builds the background and techniques needed to connect digital systems to the physical world.
Uses team-based user-centered design methods on a real-world problem to design, prototype, and evaluate solutions.
Covers organizational and digital communication, messaging, presentations, storytelling, and communication strategy.
Examines ethical and societal impacts of emerging technologies, including inclusion, bias, harm, and accountability.

core_courses.course_id: Hardware/Software Lab I (Connected Devices track only)
core_courses.course_summary: Required spring quarter lab for the Connected Devices track focused on integrating software and hardware concepts.
Required spring quarter lab for the Robotics track focused on fundamental robotics applications such as manipulation and motion planning.
Teaches user research and evaluation methods for ethically grounded design and usability assessment.
Introduces digital signal processing, machine learning, and cloud-based data storage and retrieval.
Required autumn quarter lab for the Robotics track that extends robotics application work into mobility and navigation.

core_courses.course_id: Hardware/Software Lab II (Connected devices track only)
core_courses.course_summary: Required autumn quarter lab for the Connected Devices track that extends prototyping into networking, cloud interfaces, and web-based user interfaces.

core_courses.course_id: Programming for Digital & Physical User Interfaces
core_courses.course_title: Programming for Digital & Physical User Interfaces
core_courses.course_summary: Builds programming skills for sensor-based signals and prototyping software and hardware user interfaces.
Immerses students in the theory and practice of design concepts for interactive products.
Introduces opportunity evaluation, venture creation, and startup finance basics.
Prepares students for transition to professional practice through industry trends, leadership, program management, entrepreneurship, and ethics.
Gives experience creating a product roadmap and engineering or development plan.
Teaches commercialization planning and how to turn new technologies into market-ready products.

core_courses.course_id: Corporate & IP Law for Technology Innovators
core_courses.course_title: Corporate & IP Law for Technology Innovators
core_courses.course_summary: Introduces business law, organizational structures, and the basics of intellectual property and copyright law.
Final team-based launch project focused on proof-of-concept prototyping, business case, and final solution.
Introduces business-level strategy with emphasis on competitive advantage and strategy implementation.

electives.summary: The curriculum includes limited elective flexibility rather than a fully open elective pool. Evidence says students who have completely met the MSTI prerequisites may petition to replace Programming for AI Applications with an elective, and the program also offers optional summer experiences such as internship/CPT and global technology experience or study abroad. The page otherwise presents a largely prescribed core with track-based specialization.

llm_rationales.note: Homepage describes MHCI+D as 'UW's intensive one-year master's program at the intersection of human-computer interaction, design, and technology' and highlights interdisciplinary curriculum, collaboration, immersive learning, and career preparation.
Supports interdisciplinary positioning through 'interdisciplinary program' and the joint initiative across Allen School, HCDE, iSchool, and School of Art, Art History & Design.

Supports human-computer interaction / UI-UX emphasis via 'intersection of human-computer interaction, design, and technology' and curriculum items such as usability/user research, design of interactive systems, and user interface software.

Supports professional orientation via statements about preparing students to 'launch their future-forward careers,' portfolio and professional practices, and graduates being ready for professional practice.
Homepage states MHCI+D is UW's 'intensive one-year master's program' and 'year-long immersive experience,' supporting a two-semester duration classification.
UW catalog describes the program as 'year-long' and lists a 46-credit completion requirement, consistent with roughly two standard semesters.

Used the program's stated 'Average tuition' of $61,732 and the surrounding cost section indicating this is the tuition/fees figure for the fee-based MHCI+D program. This is tuition + mandatory program/academic fees only, not housing or full COA.

Used the program-stated tuition total of $61,732 as the cross-program comparison cost in USD. The page also notes additional required quarterly fees, but the quoted average tuition figure is the clearest bundled tuition-and-mandatory-fees subtotal available on the page.
The program is described in quarter terms (pre-Autumn, Autumn, Winter, Spring, Summer), so the curriculum uses quarter units rather than semester credits.

The core curriculum is explicitly organized by quarter with a pre-Autumn immersion and fixed Autumn/Winter/Spring/Summer courses, plus a three-course capstone sequence, indicating a highly sequenced plan.
Evidence describes electives and broad interdisciplinary options, but no distinct tracks, concentrations, or formal specializations students choose among.
The source names ten required core courses with catalog codes/titles and quarter placement: HCID 501, 530, 511, 520, 521, 540, 510, 531, 561, and 541.
The page says most students take 2–3 electives over the year-long program; I used 3 as the best single estimate because the evidence gives a range rather than a fixed count.
The program is described in quarter terms (pre-Autumn, Autumn, Winter, Spring, Summer), so quarter units are the appropriate unit system.
The curriculum is explicitly quarter-ordered with a pre-Autumn immersion, Autumn/Winter/Spring core courses, and a Summer capstone, so it is highly sequenced.
Evidence describes electives and faculty from multiple concentrations, but no distinct student-chosen tracks, concentrations, or specializations are named; therefore offers_specialization is false.
The source names ten required core courses with catalog codes and titles, including the capstone sequence and portfolio course; each was transcribed directly from the evidence.

The page says most students take 2–3 electives over the year-long program. I used the lower-middle estimate of 2 because no total degree credit requirement is stated on this page and the elective count is described as a range rather than a fixed number.
Core curriculum lists Immersion Studio as HCID 501.

Catalog describes HCID 501 as orienting students to core ideas, terminology, human-centered design process, and interdisciplinary studio environment; this fits design_methods_systems rather than design_studio at 2 credits.
Catalog lists HCID 530 as Usability and User Research.
Official course title is Usability and User Research.
Program page describes the course as a lecture course providing theoretical and intellectual foundations for qualitative and quantitative research techniques to inform design projects.
Course content centers on engaging with users, empirical and analytic evaluation, interviewing, usability testing, and evaluation methods, fitting human_factors.
Outcome statements are grounded in the catalog description of methods and skills covered by the course.
Core curriculum lists the Winter course as HCID 520, User Interface Software + Technology.
Course description emphasizes interface software/hardware, tools, environments, and sensing hardware, supporting technology as primary.
The course also foregrounds historical and theoretical backdrop, supporting a secondary history/theory label.
Learning outcomes were grounded in the catalog-style description of what students will understand and be able to explain.
The program homepage identifies MHCI+D as a University of Washington program.
The homepage title and body name the program as 'MHCI+D – Human-Computer Interaction + Design' and describe it as UW's intensive one-year master's program.
UW catalog states the program of study leads to the credential 'Master Of Human-Computer Interaction And Design (fee-based)'.
The catalog explicitly labels the credential as a master's degree, supporting degree_type = master's.

The homepage says MHCI+D was founded as a joint initiative between the Paul G. Allen School of Computer Science & Engineering, Human Centered Design and Engineering, the Information School, and the School of Art, Art History & Design.
The named sponsoring units span engineering/computing and arts/design, supporting a shared-hosted model across engineering and arts units.
UW catalog footer places the University of Washington in Seattle, WA, supporting the location label Seattle, Washington, United States.

host_academic_units: Paul G. Allen School of Computer Science & Engineering

positioning.positioning_summary: MHCI+D is positioned as an intensive, year-long, interdisciplinary program at the intersection of human-computer interaction, design, technology, and research, with strong emphasis on studio-based collaboration, prototyping, and career preparation.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: MHCI+D is a one-year, quarter-structured, cohort-based program with a pre-Autumn immersion, a fixed sequence of required core studios and lectures across Autumn, Winter, Spring, and Summer, and a three-course capstone sequence. Electives are typically 2–3 courses drawn mainly from graduate offerings across campus, especially the sponsoring departments, and they support deeper exploration rather than formal tracks.

core_courses.course_summary: A five-day pre-Autumn intensive workshop that orients new students to core HCI and design ideas, terminology, and studio skills. 2 quarter credits.
learning_outcomes: Apply core ideas and terminology in human-computer interaction and design
Use key skills needed for success in a studio learning environment
Practice the human-centered design process
Work effectively in an interdisciplinary studio environment

core_courses.course_summary: Autumn lecture course (3 quarter credits) covering qualitative and quantitative research techniques to inform design projects, including user interviewing, usability testing, field deployments, heuristic evaluation, and cognitive walkthroughs.
learning_outcomes: Use qualitative and quantitative research techniques for design projects.
Conduct user interviews and gather observational data.
Plan and run usability tests and field deployments.
Apply heuristic evaluation and cognitive walkthrough methods.
Interpret findings to inform design decisions.

core_courses.course_summary: Winter core lecture course (3 credits) covering the historical and theoretical backdrop of user interface software and hardware, including windowing systems, toolkits, UI development environments, and novel input/output/environmental sensing hardware.
learning_outcomes: Understand the historical and theoretical backdrop of UI software and hardware.
Explain core UI software tools and development environments.
Describe novel input, output, and environmental sensing hardware.
Connect interface technology choices to design decisions and implementation.

core_courses.course_summary: A Winter lecture course on the historical and theoretical backdrop for implementing user interface software and hardware.
A Winter active-learning studio centered on embodied interaction, physical computing, hardware/software integration, and prototype development.
A Winter seminar that helps students define a capstone topic, form teams, and prepare a project pitch and resources for the capstone sequence.
A Spring lecture course on design thinking, design traditions, and frameworks for making values-based design decisions.
A Spring studio where students apply formative and evaluative research methods to capstone and related technology design problems.
A Spring portfolio course focused on creating an online portfolio, a presentation portfolio, and job-search professional practices.
A Summer final studio in which teams continue iterative design, prototyping, and evaluation on a comprehensive capstone project.

electives.summary: Electives are flexible rather than track-based: students may choose graduate-level classes across campus, though most electives come from the four sponsoring departments. The program says most students take 2–3 electives over the year, and examples include seminars, design, research, technology, and professional-practice courses that deepen interests and reinforce the core studio experience.
